coredata.dc:title: Antagonistic interaction of Pb2+- Al3+ ion pair with Sugar derived Carbon dots: Visual monitoring of Al 3+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A.
Renuka, N.K.
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, Maillard reaction assisted route is followed to yield carbon dots from commercialy available table sugar.The system is characterized using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray Photo electron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectroscopy (FTIR), Dynamic light scattering analysis (DLS) and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The system is found to be highly susceptible for aggregation in the presence of Pb2+ ions, which induced quenching of luminescence of these dots. The aggregation of carbon dots is destroyed and the luminescence was restored in prescence of trace amounts (parts per trillion) of Al3+ ions. This highly specific observation introduces the concept of “Ion antagonism” of the said ions. The visible re-dispersal of carbon dots in presence of Al3+ ions enables naked eye monitoring of theseions, whose quantification is achieved through simple turbidimetry. Based on this, the presence of Humic acid functionalities on the carbon core is suggested. Hence through this study, apart from the identification of a novel antagonistic ion-pair, a prediction on the surface functional groups on the carbon core is made in the case of carbon dots derived from sucrose via Maillard reaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Al 3+ions
Naked eye sensing
Humic acid
Antagonism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Al 3+ induced stability of aggregated carbon dots
Reasons for opposing action – participation of functional groups
Interaction of Al3+ ions and carbon dots
An extension to antagonistic interaction- Detection of Al3+ ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala and the facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR- National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM and EDAX analysis. Authors also thank the Centre for Nanosciences, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences, Kochi for providing the XPS data. Authors thank the Department of Nanoscience and Technology, University of Calicut for providing the DLS analysis facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), C1s (b) and O1s (c) XPS peaks, Fluorescence spectrum (λExc.350 nm) (d), fluorescence decay (lifetime) curve (λExc.370 nm) (e) and XRD pattern (f) of carbon dots.

Aggregation of carbon dot system in presence of Pb2+ in ppb level, illustarting thurbidity generation after Pb2+ (a); Antagonistic interaction –Carbon dot solution (b) - under day light (i), Turbid solution in presence of Pb2+ ions in ppb levels (ii) and loss of turbidity in presence of Al3+ ions in ppt level (iii); Corresponding images under UV radiation (c), Dynamic light scattering analysis- Hydrodynamic radius of carbon dot before and after the addition of Pb 2+ ions (d) -confirming the aggregation of carbon dots.
Spectral Evidences confirming the restoration of carbon dot character upon addition of Al3+ ions to the carbon dot-Pb2+ system. (a)Fluorescence emission spectra, (b) UV absorbance spectra.
FTIR spectrum of Table sugar derived carbon dots.

Stability and insoluble nature of aggregated particles at various pH values ruling out the possibility of aggregated humic acid and Pb-Humate in the systems (corresponding pH values are marked on the vessels).
FTIR spectra of carbon dots, before and after Pb2+addition.
Bonding between humic acid functional groups on carbon dots and Pb2+ions leading to charge neutralization and aggregation.
Schematic representation showing formation of carbon dots and its antagonistic interactions between Pb2+ and Al3+ ions.
Linear response of turbidity disappearance with concentration of added Al3+ ions.
Carbon dot –Pb2+ unit in presence of mixture of analytes; without Al3+ion (a) and with Al3+ion (b).
Comparison of carbon dot based Al 3+ sensors reported in literature.

row.entry:
Dissolution of Aggregated carbon dot
(Antagonistic interaction between Pb2+& Al 3+)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Al 3+ ions in Real water samples.

head.ce:title: Antagonistic interaction of Pb2+- Al3+ ion pair with Sugar derived Carbon dots: Visual monitoring of Al 3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala – 673 635, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, Maillard reaction assisted route is followed to yield carbon dots from commercialy available table sugar.The system is characterized using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray Photo electron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectroscopy (FTIR), Dynamic light scattering analysis (DLS) and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The system is found to be highly susceptible for aggregation in the presence of Pb2+ ions, which induced quenching of luminescence of these dots. The aggregation of carbon dots is destroyed and the luminescence was restored in prescence of trace amounts (parts per trillion) of Al3+ ions. This highly specific observation introduces the concept of “Ion antagonism” of the said ions. The visible re-dispersal of carbon dots in presence of Al3+ ions enables naked eye monitoring of theseions, whose quantification is achieved through simple turbidimetry. Based on this, the presence of Humic acid functionalities on the carbon core is suggested. Hence through this study, apart from the identification of a novel antagonistic ion-pair, a prediction on the surface functional groups on the carbon core is made in the case of carbon dots derived from sucrose via Maillard reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, the quasi spherical particles of carbon with size less than 10 nm possess intense fluorescence, which other carbon forms lack. Owing to the fluorescent nature, these tiny particles are drawing much attention in the scientific world [1–5]. Though the distinct picture regarding the structure of carbon dots still remains unveiled, these particles are conceptualized with a carbon core (either graphitic or amorphous origin), decorated with functionalities including carbonyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups. It is established that the precursors to theses dots do have a decisive role in fixing the specific functional groups on the carbon core [6,7]. Hence, carbon dots derived from different precursors and synthesis routes possess entirely different features [8]. Though there are numerous publications exploiting the fluorescence feature of carbon dots, productive efforts ending up with a scientific backup about the exact structure and fluorescence mechanism are almost nil in literature, as illustrated in various review papers [9,10]. Herein, a leap is made in this direction based on the specific interaction of Pb2+ - Al3+ ion pair with carbon dots derived from Table sugar (sucrose) through Maillard pathway.

A previous report of ours has presented visible aggregation of table sugar derived carbon dots in presence of Pb2+ ions [11]. In the present report, we demonstrate that Al3+ ions are able to revert back the aforementioned aggregation process, thus bringing back the dispersion / stability of carbon dots. As the above-mentioned processes viz., aggregation and dispersal, are two physically opposite phenomena, these two ions are said to exhibit “antagonistic” interaction with table sugar derived carbon dots. ‘Ion antagonism’, a phenomenon of high scientific interest, refers to a process where a particular action of an ion is overcome by the presence of another. Though antagonism of some metal ion pairs has been reported previously [12–14], reports on competitive interaction between Pb2+ and Al3+ ions towards any physical property are nil in literature. Further research on this observation suggested the presence of humic acid functional groups on sugar derived carbon dots. Besides, as Al3+ ions could restore the stable nature of aggregated carbon dots with all its inherent features, visible detection (both naked eye and fluorimetric) of the ion is enabled using the aggregated carbon dots (carbon dot-Pb2+ system). Quantification of Al3+ in ppt level is achieved using turbidimetry, with a limit of detection 640 ppt. Overall, the report brings forth three significant observations; (i) antagonistic behaviour of carbon dots in presence of the said ion pair, (ii) naked eye monitoring of Al3+ ions, and (iii) the identification of functional groups on these carbon dots.

Commercially available table sugar was collected from local market. Table sugar was caramelized for about 3 min by heating at 150 °C in a furnace. Caramelized sugar slurry was mixed with 20 ml of 90 % ammonia (Merck) solution. The solution thus obtained was heated at 120 °C using microwave radiation for 3 min in the reaction chamber. The pale yellow solution collected was further subjected to ultra centrifugation (15,000 rpm for 20 min). The unreacted compounds were removed by conducting dialysis for 24 h against distilled water (membrane MWCO≈1kDa).

Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope was used for HR-TEM imaging of powdered carbon dot sample. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with the aid of Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer using Al K-α X-ray beam. Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR) spectrum of the sample is obtained via KBr disc method with the aid of JASCO FTIR-4100 instrument. Uv–Visible absorbance spectrum was recorded using JASCOV-550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot was recorded with the aid of Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55. Fluorescent images were captured by placing the sample in LZC-4X photoreactor. Rigaku MiniFlex-II diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation source was adopted for X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the sample. Fluorescence decay time was monitored using Fluoro Log-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon Inc., USA), by maintaining the excitation wavelength as 370 nm. Labtronics LT-33 (Digital Turbidity Meter) was used for turbidimetric measurements. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement was conducted with the aid of Zetasizer Nano ZS-90; Malvern Instruments.

Preliminary characterization of the carbon dot system is carried out using TEM, XPS, fluorescence spectroscopy, XRD and FTIR spectroscopy techniques. A brief summary of the characterization techniques is presented in Fig. 1 . TEM analysis (Fig. 1a) confirms the formation of spherical particles with diameter in the range 3−4 nm. Lattice fringe pattern with ‘d’ spacing 0.21 nm is noted from the image. Figs. 1 (b) and 1(c) show the XPS spectra of carbon dots. The C1s and O1s spectra confirm the presence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups, marked by the characteristic peaks corresponding to CC, CC, OC = O and COC/C-O-H groups. The inherent luminescent nature of carbon dots under excitation wavelength of 350 nm is displayed in Fig. 1 (d). Inset shows the digital image of brightly fluorescent carbon dot solution under the same excitation. Fluorescence decay profile of carbon dots under excitation wavelength of 370 nm revealed an average fluorescence lifetime of 4.9 ns (Fig. 1 e). XRD pattern of carbon dots indicates highly exfoliated graphitic nature of the material (Fig. 1f), characterized by distinct bands denoting (002) and (101) planes [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al 3+ induced stability of aggregated carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of sugar derived carbon dots was clear and transparent, which exhibited the inherent luminescent feature, cyan in colour. Presence of parts per billion (ppb) levels of Pb2+ ions imparts noticeable turbidity to the carbon dot solution, as discussed in our previous report [11]. Digital image showing carbon dot solution before and after the Pb2+ addition is displayed in Fig. 2 (a). Loss of luminescence of the system was also noted in presence of Pb2+ ions. The turbidity of the solution pre-treated with Pb2+ ions disappears in presence of Al3+ ions (Fig. 2b), which can be monitored by naked eye. Concomitant with this, restoration of inherent luminescence of the carbon dots was also noticed under UV light (Fig. 2c). Dynamic light scattering analysis further confirms the aggregation of carbon dot with the advent of Pb2+ ions (Fig. 2d). The average particle size of carbon dots was observed to be 11 nm with poly dispersity index (PDI) 0.34. The increased particle size obtained via DLS is normally expected, which is explained on the basis of formation of electrical double layer on the particle, as well as the path hindrance created with overlapped nanoparticles while conducting the scattering experiment. As evident from the figure, the introduction of trace amounts of Pb2+ ions to the carbon dot solution changes the average hydrodynamic diameter of the carbon particles from 11 d.nm to 460 d.nm. This drastic increment in size of the particles further ensures the proposed Pb2+ ion induced aggregation of carbon dots. The proposed aggregation of individual carbon dot units with the introduction of Pb 2+ ions is further substantiated through the HR-TEM image that indicated coarse aggregated particles (Figure S1a). EDAX pattern of the aggregate further confirms the incorporation of Pb 2+ ions in the aggregated unit (Figure S1b). XRD pattern of carbon dot-Pb 2+ system validates the aggregation of carbon dots, which was very much similar to that of pure carbon dot system (Figure S2). The details in this regard are presented in the previous manuscript [11].

This Al3+ induced dispersal of aggregated carbon dots is further supported by the fluorescence spectrum of the solution, recorded in presence of Al3+ions (Fig. 3 a). Restoration of luminescence of carbon dots in presence of Al3+ species, in sufficient amount is thus confirmed. The UV absorbance spectrum also substantiates the argument (Fig. 3b). Visible sensing of Al3+species using UV light as well as naked eye are evident from these data. The ability of Al3+ ions to revert the aggregation of dots, achieved by Pb2+ ions; an antagonistic behaviour is thus confirmed. A preliminary conclusion can be arrived at that the stability of carbon dots lost in presence of Pb2+ ions, leading to turbidity may be regained in presence of Al3+ ions, bringing back the inherent characters of these dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reasons for opposing action – participation of functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain these observations, further deep probing was done on the structure of carbon dots, focusing on the source and the route to these dots. The search ended up in the synthetic pathway to carbon dots, the Maillard reaction, which is the amine / ammonia mediated caramelization of sugar. The role of ammonia similar to amines in reaction with reducing sugars (Maillard pathway) has been already established [16].There is still ambiguity in predicting the exact by-products/ products in this caramelization pathway. But doubtlessly, it proceeds through a path, which produced Humic acid (HA) as an intermediate [17], which is marked for the extra ordinary competitive affinity of Al3+ ions over Pb2+ ions, towards it. As the precursor / side product in the reaction mixture during carbonization has profound influence in determining the functional group on carbon dots, the presence of humic acid surface functional group on carbon dot is suggested, which shows the above-mentioned competitive interaction. The decrease in pH of the solution during the reaction supports this proposal [18]. The main constituents of Humic acids include aromatic and aliphatic structures as well as carbonyl, carboxylic and phenolic-OH groups [19]. Due to the wide variety and large number of functional groups in humic acids, particularly carboxylic and hydroxylic sites (1 mg L−1 of the species corresponds approximately to 1–5 μmolL−1complexing sites), they have high complexation ability and relative selectivity towards heavy metal ions [20], and play an important role in metal complexation in natural waters, thus strongly affecting the bioavailability of heavy metal ions. The extraordinary affinity of HA towards divalent metal ions, which neutralize the charge on HA leading to aggregation of the molecule is reported in literature [21].

This preliminary assumption is substantiated by meticulously analyzing the surface functional groups on carbon dots. An idea about the functional groups on carbon dot surface is obtained from the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4 ) of the system. The signal centered at 3440 cm−1 assigned to the OH stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of alcohols/phenols, and possibly carboxylic acids. Weak absorption bands at 2930 and 2840 cm−1 denote asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations. The broad shoulder near 1625 cm-1 due to aromatic CC conjugated to CO/COO‾ is seen merged with the CO asymmetric stretching vibration at 1540 cm−1. The intense band at ∼ 1400 cm−1 justifies the presence of COO‾ group vibration (CO symmetric stretching) and C–H deformation mode in CH3, OH deformation and CO stretch of phenols, which is a unique spectral feature of humic substances [22–25]. The absence of band at 1220 cm−1 indicates the absence of CO stretching from un-dissociated COOH groups. Besides the shift of CO bands to 1720 cm−1, infers that the carboxylic group might be strongly hydrogen bonded, which gets merged with the anti-symmetric stretching of carboxylate band, as shown by the IR spectrum. Alcoholic and polysaccharide CO stretch and OH deformation are evident by a significant band at 1035 cm−1. The stretching vibration of aromatic CH bonds, usually visible in 3200–3000 cm−1 region may be masked by the broad OH signal. All the above mentioned bands agree well with those reported for Humic acids (HA), rich with carboxylate and hydroxyl groups [26]. These conclusions are further supported by the concerned spectral details of humic acid, as reported by Rodrigues et al. [19]. This inference is further backed by the work by Dong et al. in which similar bands were obtained for humic acid functionalized carbon dots synthesized directly from humic acid [27]. Thus infra-red spectroscopy successfully confirms the presence of HA functional groups on carbon dots, due to which the particles are expected to show high affinity for Pb2+ Ions.

However, as HA themselves are insoluble at a certain range of pH as well as luminescent, the chance of free humic acid crystallization in the system is to be ruled out, if present. It is to be noted that solid aggregation and concomitant turbidity arise at pH ∼5.5 in presence of Pb2+ ions. The percentages of insoluble portion of HA are 83.9, 88, 22.2, and 7.4 at the respective pH values 1, 3, 5 and 7, as seen in literature [28]. Humic acids become completely insoluble below pH 1 and the solubility of HA is increases with increase in pH. A comparison of images of aggregated particles at different pH values (Fig. 5 ) clearly shows that the aggregates are stable at higher pH values also, excluding the possibility of free humic acid solidification in presence of Pb2+ ions. Similarly, another probable candidate, Pb-Humate also can be ruled out as it exists in solid state in aqueous solutions in the pH range 3.5–5 only [29].The particular aggregation behaviour was observed at pH 5.5, which naturally excludes the possibility of Pb hydroxide formation, which is usually expected at higher pH values of the solution. Absence of crystalline phases other than disordered carbon framework as evident from XRD pattern is also worth remembering here. XRD pattern of humic acid crystals also does not match with that of the aggregates [30]. All these observations confirm the aggregation of carbon dots in presence of Pb2+ ions, where the humic acid surface functional groups take a lead role in the aggregation.

Quite a good amount of work has been carried out on the interaction of humic substances with metal ions, all of which indicating the special affinity of HA towards Pb2+ [31–35]. Studies on the interactions between humic substances and heavy metals show that the stability sequence of metal ion complexes with HA under the given conditions among a group of divalent metal ions (Cu, Zn, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Pb) were in the order, Co(II) < Ni(II) < Zn(II) < Cu(II) <Pb(II) [31]. The different affinity of ions towards humic ligand can be explained by their chemical properties such as the size of ion radius (r), charge (z) of the metal ion, that is ionic potential, Ip = z/r, (ii) the electro negativity of the element and (iii) ligand field stabilization energy effects and classification, which is based on the electron configuration of the cations and the concept of hard/soft acids and bases (HSAB theory) [32–34]. The tendency of cations to form inner-sphere complexes with humic acid increases with increasing ionic potential (Ip), which is valid for some metal ions, but not for Pb (II), which possessed low ionization potential. The higher stability constant obtained for complex between humic acid and Pb2+ can be explained based on the HSAB theory. Humic acids behaving as weak acid poly-electrolytes with a variety of oxygen containing functional groups with oxygen as a donor atom show hard base properties (high electronegative atom) and make strong bonds with hard acids. Metal ions like Cu(II) and Zn(II) etc., classified as soft acids,do not form stable complexes with O-donor ligands, and lead to weak interaction with humic substances. Pb2+ ions, despite its low ionization potential, show great affinity to humic acid due to its borderline acid properties, that results in greater stability of the Pb(II)––Humate moiety compared to other complexes. Higher affinity of humic group to Pb2+ ions, when compared to other metal ions is suggested by Xiong et al. too [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of Al3+ ions and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of Pb2+ ions on humic acid in presence of Al3+ ions has been a topic of interest for the researchers as it is reflected in the bioavailability and toxicity of Pb2+ ions. On a preliminary examination, the higher affinity of Al3+ ions towards humic acid functionality can be explained by the highly specific hard-hard interaction between oxygen rich humic group and Al3+ ions. The specific competition between Al3+ and Pb2+was studied by Mota et al. in view of the fact that presence of competitive interaction of metal ions influence the bioavailability of ions [38]. He suggested that the interaction between Pb2+ ions and humic acid is almost instantaneous, and is weakened in presence of Al3+ ions, which was the exact observation obtained here in this study. Highly specific interaction of Pb2+ with humic substances and the competitive binding of Al3+ on humic group is reported by Pinheiro et al. also [39]. Santos et al. have pin pointed the higher affinity of Al3+ ions towards humic acid when compared to Pb2+ [31]. While studying the complexation capacity of humic substances with some metal ions, they showed that Al3+ has the maximum complexing ability among the selected group, which showed the following order, Cr(VI)< Cd(II)<Pb(II)<Al(III). Researchers have proved that competition of Al3+ ions is the result of electrostatic influence of Al3+, that affects the overall charge of the system, and noted that Al3+ competition reduces the amount of Pb2+specifically bound to carboxylic type sites [38,39].Humic moiety can bind the free metal cation (Al3+) as well as the first hydrolysis product (AlOH2+). In the specific pH range below 6, the trivalent cation, Al3+ is found to be the dominant form bound on the humic part [40]. When Al3+ replaces Pb2+, the excess unbalanced positive charge keeps the system re-dispersed and stable, which explains the loss of aggregation. These observations clearly support the antagonistic interaction of sugar derived carbon dots towards Pb2+ and Al3+ ions. The schematic representation showing the pathway to sugar derived carbon dots and the antagonistic nature is provided in Fig. 8 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: An extension to antagonistic interaction- Detection of Al3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of Al 3+ ions to destruct the aggregated carbon dot paves way to a simple sensor for the said species. The concentration dependant optimization study confirms that the turbidity formed vanished linearly with the increase in Al3+ concentration. Quantification of Al3+ ions is possible by measuring the turbidity of the solution using a simple turbidimeter. Fig. 9 presented in this regard reveals that the reduction in turbidity with the amount of the analyte ion is perfectly linear, with regression coefficient 0.997. The limit of detection achieved using the said method is found to be 640 ppt, a remarkable achievement while considering the benchmark value prescribed by US-EPA guidelines as well as the detection limit allowed by Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [41].

The excellent selectivity of the system towards trivalent aluminium ions is illustrated as follows. In order to test the interference of various analytes in the dispersal of aggregated carbon dot units, the carbon dot-Pb2+ mixture is treated with a mixture of analytes with and without Al 3+ ions (Fig. 10 ). Aggregated carbon dot fails to dissolve when the carbon dot-Pb2+ combination is exposed to a mixture of ions devoid of Al 3+ (Fig.10a), whereas the cloudy solution becomes transparent while treating with an analyte mixture including trivalent Aluminium, in sufficient amount (Fig.10 b).This observation illustrates the extreme sensitive and significantly selective action of Al 3+ ions towards the aggregated carbon dot units under complicated chemical environments. The influence of experimental pH on Al 3+ sensing efficiency was investigated by conducting the entire experiment under different pH conditions. The system performed successfully in a wide range of pH of the solutions ranging from 2 to 12 (Fig S3), as noted from the re-dispersal of the aggregate under the above mentioned pH values.

There are only a few articles that report carbon dot based Al3+ sensors, all of which exploit the luminescence feature of carbon nanodots for tracing the presence of this analyte. Table 1 summarises all the reports in this regard [42–47]. Among the reports, the system reported by sooksin etal [47] is the only one which dominates the present sugar based carbon dot in its efficiency. The rest of them are marked with interference with analyte ions [42,45], tedious chemical procedure [46] and compulsory surface modification [42,44,45], which of course fade their charm. Though the sucrose-urea derived carbon dot synthesised through Fe3+catalyzed reaction, mentioned above [47] excels our system in sensitivity (detection limit of 9.6 nM), the demand for instrumental assistance (Flourimeter) for tracing Al 3+ ions sets a demerit, when compared to the features of present Al3+sensing tool which enables naked eye identification of Al3+ ions. It can be seen that the effortless sensing strategy of the present system definitely permits its service even to a layman.

The practical applicability of the proposed sugar based sensor was investigated by searching the presence of the analyte ion in real water samples. Study conducted using water samples spiked with Al3+ ions show the reliability of the present sensor (Table 2 ). Well water and tap water collected from local premises, spiked with Al3+ ions (represented as sample A and sample B respectively) were tested for this. The results show that the spiked recovery percentages of real water analysis fall in the commendable (98.8–99.5) range, confirming its practical utility.

In brief, this paper reports the behaviour of sugar derived carbon dots,which exhibit opposing actions, vis., aggregation and re-dispersal in presence of Pb2+ and Al3+ ions, respectively. This phenomenon thus reveals a novel antagonistic ion pair, Pb2+- Al3+. Further deep probing into this ion pair interaction leads to an idea of functionality on the carbon core, atleast in the case of carbon dots derived from sucrose via Maillard reaction. This specific opposing feature between these two metal ions is attributed to humic functional groups on carbon dot surface, which was confirmed by FTIR spectral data.The possibilities of formation of chemical species other than carbon dots during the aggregation process were ruled out by systematic analysis of the solid obtained upon Pb2+ addition. Besides, naked eye monitoring of Al3+ ions with remarkable detection limit (640 ppt), is also enabled with the aid of these carbon dots.
V.A. Ansi: Investigation. N.K. Renuka: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala and the facilities received from the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut. The authors thank CSIR- National Chemical Laboratory, Pune for providing the HRTEM and EDAX analysis. Authors also thank the Centre for Nanosciences, Amrita Institute of Medical Sciences, Kochi for providing the XPS data. Authors thank the Department of Nanoscience and Technology, University of Calicut for providing the DLS analysis facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.124632.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
